However, if the price increase in the far-month contract leads to a narrowing of the spread, the global economy will face catastrophic consequences. Forget about the war of words between Trump and the Iranian Revolutionary Guard; just focus on this chart. Currency Quantity vs. Currency Price After the outbreak of war, the two-year Treasury yield (white) surged above the effective federal funds rate (gold). This indicates that the market believes the Federal Reserve will raise interest rates to combat energy inflation. It's time to take sides. When pricing Bitcoin, do you think the quantity of money or the price of money is more important? I believe the quantity of money determines the price of Bitcoin. Bitcoin has no cash flow, so the discount rate derived from central bank policy rates is meaningless for valuing this magical internet currency. However, given that Bitcoin's supply is fixed, its fiat value depends on the total stock of fiat currency. Making this judgment is crucial because I think we may be entering a situation where major central banks, including the Federal Reserve, may be raising interest rates while simultaneously printing money directly or through the commercial banking system. As war drives up food and energy prices, capable governments will subsidize the prices of core economic inputs to avoid social unrest or famine. However, to prevent widespread inflation, central banks must suppress demand in credit-sensitive sectors by raising interest rates, thus destroying aggregate demand. Those who rely on borrowing for consumption will reduce spending when credit costs rise. If central banks only do this, my Bitcoin prediction is simple: Bitcoin prices will fall as people cut back on spending across the board, except for food and energy. But regardless of whether they are friend or foe to the American system, all countries must increase defense spending and stockpile key commodities. Do you want your country to be like Australia, which is almost 100% reliant on imports from China for refined hydrocarbons? China stopped exporting at the start of the war, while Australia had less than a month's worth of reserves. They had to beg Singapore and pay an inexorably high price for jet fuel, otherwise all those "country bumpkins" would be stuck on the Australian continent forever... I know some of you will gloat over this, especially Japanese skiers. Manufacturing bombs (especially nuclear bombs) to avoid becoming a junk-stan, coupled with stockpiling commodities, requires massive government borrowing. If domestic private investors are unwilling or unable to buy these junk bonds, central banks and/or commercial banks must print money to buy them, thereby expanding the fiat currency supply. The coexistence of rising central bank policy rates and an expanding money supply will lead to a divergence in the prices of risky assets. Assets valued based on discounted cash flow will plummet as currency prices rise. Assets with fixed or near-fixed supply, such as Bitcoin and gold, will rise, as central banks and commercial banks expand credit to finance wars and commodity stockpiles. Keep this logic in mind when reading my assessments of Bitcoin in various scenarios. You must judge the relative importance of quantity versus price in currency, otherwise you cannot understand the seemingly contradictory price movements of different risky assets. Bitcoin Outlook in Various Scenarios Return to Normalcy Bitcoin may see a slight rebound after the situation returns to pre-war levels. However, the AI-driven deflationary bomb is still ticking in the shadows. Unless the Federal Reserve provides liquidity to fill the black hole in bank balance sheets caused by consumer credit defaults, Bitcoin will not see a meaningful rise. This doesn't mean it can't reach $80,000-$90,000, but for me, to invest new fiat currency, I must wait for the Federal Reserve to signal a complete reversal of the alarm. I've already heavily invested in long positions because my strategy is purely long. I'm happy to see my account equity rise, but from a risk-reward perspective, now is not the time to significantly increase positions and push the portfolio to the highest risk level. I don't know how long it will take for the banking system to collapse. But every week I see news of massive layoffs at companies due to AI agents being far more efficient than ordinary humans, and news of rising consumer credit default rates. Here's an anecdote: I recently met a fellow entrepreneur who runs a successful crypto game company and is a veteran in the industry. We talked about the impact of AI on his business. He's a computer engineer by training, and during the 2025 Christmas holiday, he tried developing with the latest version of Claude and was amazed by its ability to quickly generate deployable code. So he held an offline meeting with his core engineers to discuss the impact of AI on the business over the next few months. He had them build an autonomous agent workflow, allowing AI to code 24/7. They automated code reviews, and every morning they woke up to usable, tested code waiting for senior engineers to review. One employee, with AI assistance, completed a roadmap that would normally take six months in just four days. After the meeting, my friend decided the company had to immediately adjust its workflow, resulting in 50% of the workforce facing extinction within the next few weeks. In the era of AI agents, ordinary engineers are worthless, while top talents will see their efficiency increase by 10 to 100 times with the help of AI. As models acquire more specialized domain knowledge, all mediocre knowledge workers face the risk of unemployment. Unfortunately, despite unemployment insurance, according to data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics and the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, the median annual maximum unemployment benefit in various U.S. states is approximately $28,000, far below the median wage of knowledge workers, which is $85,000 to $90,000. They have no choice but to default on consumer loans. The fiat fiat currency fractional-reserve banking system has reached its end. Bitcoin (yellow) / Gold vs. US Software SaaS ETF IGV (white) Even so, after the ceasefire, US SaaS software stocks resumed their decline, while Bitcoin held its ground and rebounded. This break in correlation is encouraging, but in my opinion, it's premature to declare that Bitcoin has ignored the deflationary pressures on knowledge workers caused by AI and to foreshadow a significant surge. As countries sell off dollar assets to raise yuan to pay tolls, US Treasury bonds and stocks will fall. This process may be slow because there are currently other payment options besides the yuan. However, given the leverage within the system, even a small snowflake could trigger a financial avalanche—selling triggers more selling, volatility soars, and markets freeze. At that point, monetary officials will have to intervene by printing money. A key indicator to watch is the MOVE index (US bond market volatility index). When this index breaks through 130, some form of money printing will occur. Bitcoin is unlikely to rebound significantly during periods of rising volatility and declines in large US tech stocks. Investors will sell Bitcoin to meet margin requirements as they seek to mitigate risk due to rising volatility and falling prices. Bitcoin will only rise when the situation deteriorates to a certain extent and a consensus forms in the market regarding the need for a bailout.

Just wait for Bessant or the next Fed Chair to press the printing press button.The risk-reward ratio of betting in advance is not worthwhile. I hope that Bitcoin can hold $60,000 when traditional finance collapses completely. If Bitcoin tests and holds this level a second time, I will generally be inclined to add to my position.

Stars and Stripes block the Strait of Hormuz & Empire bombs again

If the price of crude oil futures in the far month quickly catches up with the spot or near month price, the global economy will suffer a severe blow. Demand destruction will ultimately severely damage US Treasury bonds and US stocks. Similar to the previous scenario, Bitcoin's initial reaction is a decline. Once the highly leveraged Western financial system collapses, the printing press will run wildly. If the blockade ultimately ends with punitive bombing of Iran and the destruction of all energy production capacity in the Persian Gulf, it could lead to the destruction of the Iranian state. The Bitcoin rally driven by expectations of money printing may be short-lived, as the destruction of Iran would significantly increase the risk of World War III. Portfolio Construction As a pure long-only investor without leverage, Maelstrom can rely on time and compound interest to grow his portfolio. The slight outperformance of Bitcoin relative to IGV over the past few days has been encouraging, prompting me to reassess my pessimistic view of Bitcoin in the context of accelerating deflation among AI knowledge workers. Currently, the only assets I'm willing to add to are gold and HYPE (Hyperliquid governance token). The HIP-4 proposal will be launched in a few weeks, and I expect it to take a significant market share from Polymarket and Kalshi in the prediction market space. Besides this, all I can do every day is pray to Satoshi Nakamoto: that He might influence the minds of the world's political elites, leading them to swallow hallucinogenic drugs instead of dropping bombs.

data.title: Who can figure out the "Token Bill"?
data.description:
Author: Xiaojing, Tencent Technology

Computational power currency Tokens are reshaping the value coordinates of the AI ​​era, but while they are transparent, their value remains a black box.The AI ​​industry is still searching for a true anchor that can price results.

In March, Huang Renxun stood on the stage of GTC 2026 and described a new industrial era driven by tokens: AI factories continuously produce tokens, while agentic AI pushes the demand for inference to new peaks.

In March, Huang Renxun stood on the stage of GTC 2026 and described a new industrial era driven by tokens: AI factories continuously produce tokens, while agentic AI pushes the demand for inference to new peaks.

To put it simply, tokens seem like the most standard, unified, and quantifiable economic unit in the AI ​​era. Looking at the official pricing page of large models, we see a near-industry-standard uniformity: a clearly marked price per million tokens, with scales for input, output, caching, and batch processing. This high degree of uniformity in format often creates the illusion that the AI ​​industry has entered a competitive stage of mature rules and standardized products, with tokens being the most important metric of this era. However, the reality is quite the opposite. In April 2026, the input price of OpenAI's flagship model GPT-5.4 was $2.5 per million tokens, and the output price was $15, compared to $5 and $25 for Anthropic's flagship model Claude Opus 4.6, respectively. Judging solely from the listed prices, Anthropic was twice as expensive. However, for any enterprise technology leader, simply comparing these two numbers is meaningless. The differences between the two companies in context window strategies, tool call billing logic, and cache discount depth are enough to erase or even invert the price multiplier relationship. The price of a token is indeed transparent, but the "value" behind the token price is a black box. How much one must pay to obtain the same value is still difficult to define clearly. The pricing of a token itself is not mysterious. A developer who has long used APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) stated, "The price of a token is not a black box, and manufacturers cannot fake it. The number of tokens input and output can be estimated; even if the tokenizer is not public, it can be roughly calculated." [Image: Price comparison between GPT5.4 and Opus4.6] Economically, this is similar to a typical "homogeneous unit of measurement," like kilowatt-hours for electricity or gigabytes for storage. Tokens provide a standardized consumption metric, allowing buyers and sellers to trade on the same scale. The problem lies in the fact that tokens are not just traditional units of measurement; they measure intelligence and promise value. Users buy tokens to acquire the "intelligence" of the model, which can truly become a productive force. This includes writing runnable code, completing a customer service conversation, or producing data analysis. The exchange rate—how much capability each token can acquire—is the real black box. Furthermore, developers have reported that "sometimes, such as before a new model is released, the model vendor will reduce its intelligence and thinking intensity. This is a black box, but users can perceive it." In early April 2026, Stella Laurenzo, AMD's AI Strategy Director, released an analysis on GitHub based on 6852 Claude Code sessions. The data showed that starting in late February 2026, the inference depth of Claude Opus 4.6 decreased significantly—"reads-per-edit" (the number of file reads before each code edit) plummeted from 6.6 to 2.0, a decrease of approximately 67%. In other words, the model started modifying the code without carefully reading it. Laurenzo concluded: "When thinking becomes superficial, the model tends to take the lowest-cost action: modifying without reading, stopping before completion, shirking responsibility for errors, and choosing the simplest solution rather than the most correct one." On April 14th, media reports revealed another side of the story. Boris Cherny, the creator of Claude Code, responded that Opus 4.6 enabled "adaptive thinking" by default starting February 9th, and further adjusted the default effort level from high to medium (85%) on March 3rd. Anthropic believed this was "the best balance between intelligence, latency, and cost for most users." Users can manually type /effort high to restore full reasoning. The problem is: these changes were not communicated to users in any prominent way. Many developers only began to suspect that "the model has become stupid" after the code quality had clearly deteriorated. However, this is difficult to prove. A simple "inconsistent testing environment" perfectly suffices to address the doubts. "The probabilistic nature of large models makes it difficult to prove certain things." This is the most important and most difficult-to-price variable in token economics: consuming the same 1 million tokens can yield completely different inference quality. This varies between peak and off-peak periods, between default configurations and manually increasing effort, and between when subscribers are nearing their quota limits and when quotas are plentiful. The quantity and price of tokens are transparent, but how much "intelligence" is contained within them is unknown to users and cannot be predetermined. There's an economic concept called "hedonic adjustment," which states that when the quality of a product changes, even if the nominal price remains the same, the real price has changed. Tokens face this dilemma: the listed price and quantity remain the same, but the "value" may quietly diminish. This is more insidious and harder to trace than price increases.

II. The "Cache Hit Rate" that Affects Prices

Besides the black box of fluctuations in "intellectual content," there is a more hidden cost structure beneath the price list.

In February 2026, an update to Claude Code caused a significant drop in the cache hit rate of third-party platforms. This led to questions about whether Anthropic intentionally disrupted the caching of third-party models.

An engineer used AI tools to download the source code of 11 versions of Claude Code, from v2.1.0 to v2.1.41, and analyzed them one by one. The conclusion was: there was no deliberate logic in the code to disrupt third-party models.

However, starting with v2.1.23, Claude Code introduced a Claude-specific chunked caching mechanism. Optimizations like "cross-session global sharing and 1-hour validity" altered the structure of the system prompt. Third-party model APIs couldn't recognize these markers and could only rely on basic prefix matching. Prefixes, however, are highly unstable due to continuous changes in version numbers, build times, and A/B test variables. In simpler terms, Anthropic didn't intentionally "poison" the system, but in optimizing its own model's efficiency, it inadvertently disrupted the caching conditions that third-party models relied on. While not intentional, this incident highlights a crucial point: cache hit rate determines how much you pay for tokens. A developer's tracking of Claude Code usage data over a week showed that, under normal circumstances, 91% of tokens came from cache hits, with cache hit prices only one-tenth of the standard input price. If the cache were completely invalidated, input costs would skyrocket to 5.7 times the original cost. Boris Cherny, the creator of Claude Code, himself admitted: "When using a 1M context window, the cost of cache misses is very high. If you leave your computer for more than an hour and then resume an old session, you usually won't get a cache hit at all." There are also more noteworthy details. An analysis circulating in the community claims that Claude Code silently downgrades the cache duration from 1 hour to 5 minutes after detecting that a user has entered "Extra Usage" mode. In other words, if you stop for more than 5 minutes, a complete context rebuild will be triggered, and the cost will be deducted directly from the excess balance. According to media reports, in April, some Pro users reported that they could only post two hints on Claude Code within 5 hours. One user bluntly stated, "Until these caching bugs are fixed, any discussion about 5-minute or 1-hour TTL is pointless, because the numbers are completely wrong." The example of cache hit rate illustrates that even with the same result (value), the price paid can fluctuate wildly. III. The Budget Quagmire Behind the 300x Price Drop According to industry data, the price per token has fallen by approximately 300 times in three years, but companies' AI spending has become even more difficult to control. The reason is simple: the price drop couldn't keep up with the explosive growth in usage.

Figure: Over three years, the price of LLM Tokens has fallen by approximately 300 times—but this collapse in price has not made corporate AI spending more predictable. (Source: TokenCost)

Agent-based applications enable AI to autonomously execute complex, long-chain tasks, with a single transaction potentially costing dozens of times more than traditional dialogue.

... The six-person team at Branch8, an e-commerce technology company in the Asia-Pacific region, spent $2,400 in the first month after implementing Claude Code. After eight weeks of intensive optimization, including setting daily token quotas, limiting budget for thinking patterns, and switching non-critical tasks from Opus to Sonnet, they barely managed to reduce the cost to $680. Token spending management itself has become a job requiring specialized skills. At the Nutanix 2026 .NEXT conference, a CIO shared an even more extreme case: a developer incurred an unexpected $100,000 token bill, and had to explain it to an unprepared CFO—in his own words, it was an "extremely embarrassing meeting." Another attendee mentioned that some companies are already issuing daily token quotas to employees, "like a rationing system." A survey of 372 companies by Mavrik and Benchmarkit confirms this prevalence: 84% of companies reported that AI costs eroded their gross margins more than expected, and only 15% were able to keep budget errors within 10%. In economics, this is a classic example of "unit failure." When a unit of measurement cannot accurately reflect costs (such as implicit variables like caching and desensitization) or value, and the same token produces vastly different results in different scenarios, it loses its basic function as a measure and cannot help the market form consensus or reduce transaction friction. From another perspective, the surge in token consumption remains a core narrative for model companies. However, token growth is also a cost story. OpenAI's inference costs reached $8.4 billion in 2025 and are projected to rise to $14.1 billion in 2026, with total cash burn of approximately $17 billion. It has signed cloud infrastructure contracts worth over $500 billion. Anthropic has raised over $64 billion in total funding. Neither company is currently profitable. OpenAI raised $122 billion in April 2026 at a valuation of $852 billion, while Anthropic raised $30 billion in February at a valuation of $380 billion. Investors hope to see the computing power cost per token continue to decrease, making "selling tokens" a profitable business. However, Amazon initially sold standardized goods and cloud services, with a relatively stable unit economic model. AI companies sell tokens, something seemingly standardized but actually highly heterogeneous, and these heterogeneous components (intelligence content, caching efficiency, task adaptability) are precisely the core variables affecting cost and value. Whether the "scale effect" will materialize as expected is even more uncertain than in the e-commerce and cloud computing eras.

IV. The Industry is Searching for a Pricing "Anchor"

Returning to the core question: Who can accurately calculate the value of a token?

In the short term, no one can. The price of a token is transparent, but the intellectual quality it exchanges for fluctuates. The real cost behind it is layered by caching, framework design, and computing power efficiency, and the business value it generates varies depending on the scenario.

A unit of measurement bearing so many dimensions of uncertainty inherently indicates that it cannot yet become the standard of measurement for the AI ​​era.

Tokens have not become a commodity that can be priced in a standardized way. They are a temporary unit of accounting that everyone has to use before the AI ​​industry finds a value anchor.

Currently, the industry's pricing of tokens is essentially pricing the "right to use computing power"—buying the opportunity for the model to "think" for you. How deep or good that thinking is, and whether it ultimately solves your problem, is not within the scope of this price promise. The rationality of this pricing method is currently impossible for any single party to independently judge. Vendors cannot measure the business value of output for users, users cannot penetrate the model's reasoning process to determine whether each token is "worth its price," and investors see the consumption growth curve but not the value conversion rate of each token. Ultimately, what can truly determine the value of tokens may be discovering the "unit of output" that customers are willing to pay for, defining the true productivity of intelligence, and internally managing the conversion relationship between token costs and computing power costs to a predictable level.

data.title: Allbirds, the trendy sneaker brand that swept Silicon Valley, has also gone all-in on AI.
data.description:
Author: seed.eth, BitpushNews

Leonardo DiCaprio invested, Obama and Cook competed to wear it, Emma Watson actively endorsed it…

However, this "top middle-class brand" is not selling well.

On April 15, 2026, Allbirds, a popular American sneaker brand, announced a major development: the company would completely abandon its shoe manufacturing business and fully shift its focus to artificial intelligence computing infrastructure, renaming itself "NewBird AI". Following the announcement, the stock price surged from less than $3 to a high of over $24 during the day, a single-day increase of over 800%. Just two weeks ago, this once-star brand sold all its intellectual property and footwear assets for a paltry $39 million—a mere one percent of its peak market capitalization of $4.1 billion. From wool shoes worn by Silicon Valley elites to a shell company turning to tinkering with GPU computing power—Allbirds' story is more than just the rise and fall of a startup; it reveals the madness of today's capital markets: AI is a panacea. The Rise and Fall of the "Silicon Valley Miracle Shoes" In 2015, former New Zealand professional football player Tim Brown and renewable resources expert Joey Zwillinger founded Allbirds in San Francisco. Their vision was simple and clear: to create a comfortable shoe that didn't rely on petroleum-based raw materials, using natural materials such as merino wool and eucalyptus fiber. In 2016, their first product, the Wool Runner, was launched and quickly became a hit in Silicon Valley's tech circles. Google co-founder Larry Page, Apple CEO Tim Cook, and even former US President Barack Obama became fans of these wool shoes. Allbirds caught two excellent trends. First, the golden age of the DTC (Direct-to-Consumer) model—bypassing traditional retail channels, reaching consumers directly through the official website, and gaining complete user data and brand narrative control. Second, the ethical awakening of "sustainable consumption"—with environmental protection becoming a global consensus, a pair of shoes with a "zero carbon footprint" is itself a declaration of values. When these two narratives took root in the fertile ground of an economic upswing, Allbirds quickly grew from a Kickstarter crowdfunding project into a publicly traded company valued at over $4 billion. However, Allbirds' fall was almost as swift as its rise. Its business collapse followed a typical DTC (Direct-to-Consumer) script: a single blockbuster product supported the entire brand, and before its foundation was solid, it rushed into apparel and brick-and-mortar retail, resulting in an overextended strategy and a loss of brand focus. As more and more brands began to talk about environmental protection, and as competitors like Hoka and On surpassed it in performance and design, Allbirds' sustainability narrative was rapidly diluted. In 2022, the company's revenue reached a historical peak of $298 million; however, it subsequently declined steadily, falling to $152 million by 2025, nearly halved. Over the past five years, despite accumulating approximately $1.2 billion in sales, total losses reached a staggering $419 million. In 2024, the company received a delisting warning from Nasdaq due to its stock price remaining below $1 for 30 consecutive days, subsequently managing to maintain its listing status through a reverse stock split. In February 2026, Allbirds announced the closure of all its full-price retail stores in the United States. On March 30, 2026, Allbirds signed an agreement with brand management company American Exchange Group to sell its intellectual property and related assets for $39 million. The buyer owns brands such as Aerosoles and Ed Hardy and will continue to sell footwear products under the Allbirds name. The price has also been reduced. I checked the prices on the website today, and shoes that used to cost over $100 are now discounted to around $30... The listed company's "shell" awaits its next fate—and the answer came faster than anyone expected. From selling shoes to buying GPUs, the announcement on April 15th can be described as one of the most unexpected transformations in business history. Allbirds announced that it had reached a convertible bond financing agreement of up to $50 million with an institutional investor. The funds will be used to acquire high-performance GPU hardware and provide computing power access services to customers through a long-term leasing model. The company plans to change its name to "NewBird AI," with a long-term vision of becoming a "fully integrated GPU-as-a-Service (GPUaaS) and AI-native cloud solutions provider." The market reaction was almost frenzied. At the close of trading on April 14th, Allbirds' market capitalization was only about $21 million; after the announcement, the stock price briefly touched $24.31, and the market capitalization ballooned to approximately $165 million. On Fidelity Investments' trading activity list, Allbirds became one of the most actively traded stocks that day, demonstrating the enthusiasm of retail traders. This frenzied market reaction was less about pricing in NewBird AI's fundamentals and more about pricing in the "AI" label itself. Allbirds' transformation announcement revealed no specific information about customer resources, the technology team, or data center deployment plans—aside from $50 million in cash and a vague blueprint of "buying GPUs and then leasing them out." Independent consultant Bruce Winder commented, "I don't think Allbirds can bring anything substantial, except for brand awareness." It's worth noting that while shifting towards AI, Allbirds is also requesting shareholder approval in its SEC filings to amend its articles of incorporation to remove the phrase "serving the public interest"—meaning that this company, once proud of its B Corp certification, is actively shedding its environmental mission. From "saving the planet" to "selling computing power," Allbirds' value leap may be more symbolic than the business transformation itself. The AI ​​narrative remains the most powerful business magic. Allbirds is not the first, and certainly won't be the last. Over the past 18 months, from fast fashion to fresh food e-commerce, from logistics companies to home furnishing brands, a large number of traditional enterprises have rushed to label themselves as "AI." The reason is actually quite simple: the price-to-earnings ratio of shoe sellers is only slightly over 10, while that of computing power sellers can be inflated to over 50; GPUs are now a hard currency, more sought after than gold, and whoever has priority in delivery holds the key to resale profits; in addition, consumers' wallets are indeed thinner, so instead of continuing to spend money on advertising and competing with Temu for traffic, it's better to step back and gamble on enterprise-level AI computing power leasing—at least the story sounds better. Looking at history in a broader context, this kind of "changing clothes" is not new. During the crypto boom of 2017, a beverage company, Long Island Iced Tea, changed its name to "Long Blockchain Corp.", and its stock price surged by nearly 300% in one day, only to be delisted from Nasdaq the following year. In 2024, many Bitcoin mining companies shifted their focus to AI data centers, with Core Scientific being one of the most successful examples. From the dot-com bubble to blockchain, and now to AI, the capital market's script has never changed: the market is priced before profitability, and the narrative precedes reality. Allbirds' transformation essentially involved exchanging its remaining brand reputation and listed company status for a GPU procurement contract. The core issue is whether this entry ticket is truly valuable. AI infrastructure is a highly capital-intensive industry with extremely high technological barriers. The GPU leasing market already has players valued at billions of dollars, as well as deep involvement from massive cloud service providers like Amazon AWS and Microsoft Azure. Whether a company that once made shoes can survive in this crowded market with only $50 million in funding and a single GPU setup remains a huge unknown. Not to mention, this funding still needs approval at the special shareholder meeting on May 18th. Bloomberg industry analyst Poonam Goyal commented, "This move allows it to exit a structurally low-margin footwear and apparel business and enter a higher-value computing power business, but the execution risk remains high." We are witnessing a footnote to an era: any entity—whatever it was before—can be redefined as an AI company. As long as the story is compelling enough, capital will buy it. The AI ​​narrative remains the most powerful business magic today.
